calls.location: Powderhorn Park, Minneapolis, Hennepin, Minnesota
calls.remarks:
Calls from an autumn flock of approximately 50 birds in a urban park. In addition to the standard 'caw' calls, there are several rattles, and a few soft, doubled hooting calls (e.g. 0:58, 1:06, 3:31) from a bird directly above my head.

Recorded with internal mic of Olympus LS-10S. High pass filter @ 500Hz.

calls.location: Powderhorn Park, Minneapolis, Hennepin, Minnesota
calls.remarks:
Calls from a flock of approximately 30 birds in a large elm tree in residential neighborhood.  

Windy. Recorded with parabolic mic and Olympus LS-10S.

calls.location: Powderhorn Park, Minneapolis, Hennepin, Minnesota
calls.remarks: A small group of crows (10-15) perched above me in a large cottonwood tree and screaming down at me.  Periodically one or two would launch into the air and circle above me while calling.  Several others flew in to join the scolding as time went by.

calls.location: Olympia, Thurston County, Washington

calls.location: Turkey Hill (near  Arlington), Middlesex County, Massachusetts
calls.remarks: Initially heard calling from trees ~30 feet away.  Two chickadees flew closer and landed on a fence about 8 feet in front of me.  They were mostly silent on the fence, then one went into a rhododendron several feet to my left and continued calling from inside the bush.  Recorded with an iPhone SE.  Several other birds overlap in the sample.  Chickadee calls at 00:00, 00:28, 00:32.

calls.location: Four Mile Draw Rd, Custer State Park, South Dakota
calls.remarks:
A bird drumming and calling from a trunk of dead ponderosa pine on a grassy hill next to a gravel road. Overcast, with light rain which can be heard hitting the microphone.

This area appears to have suffered a fire in recent years since all the trees in this area were dead trunks. As a result, this area held a large number of woodpeckers, including hairy, lewis's, red-headed, and northern flicker.

Huehuetenango, Chiantla, Huehuetenango Department
Dechambeau Creek, NW Mono Basin, Mono County, California
songs.remarks: Jay singing, loaded with mimicry. Listening to it several times in headphones, I make out mimicry of flicker, California Ground-squirrel, yellow-headed blackbird (or GT grackle), RB sapsucker, House Sparrow, BB magpie, Lesser goldfinch, a single utterance of part of a Dusky Flycatcher song. And more.

songs.type: call, song, whisper type creak song

songs.remarks: Recorded as video on Nikon D7200 camera with factory built in mic.  (.mov file )Converted to .mp3 using Zamzar online file conversion. Bird perched about 5 m up  in yard tree.

songs.location: Rustler Park, Chiricahua Mountains, Cochise County, Arizona
songs.remarks: I used playback to draw the bird in for close views and then it began this whisper song. Recording amplified with Audacity.

calls.location: McClellan Ranch Preserve, Santa Clara, California
calls.remarks: Foraging in undergrowth along the edge of a creek.

calls.location: Big Bear, San Bernardino County, California
calls.remarks: Residential area of pines and junipers, bird came down to a dripping water faucet, took some sips, fluttered wings as if begging or bathing (but not sufficient water) while making this begging-like call, then flew to lowest branch of juniper tree and continued this call while fluttering wings in a begging behavior. Unfortunately I'm not sure whether it was an adult or immature. It appeared to be an adult and it would be unlikely to have a near-adult-plumage bird in May, but the field guide says juvenile are possible May and after and I didn't get on the key field marks of the yellow bill base of a juvenile or the subtle thin blue eyebrow of an adult pacific.

species.mnemonic: Scratchy, buzzy, metallic series of chips and squeals — surprisingly loud for its size

sound_types.song: Complex scratchy buzzing and squealing song, often from an exposed perch
sound_types.call: Sharp chip note; also a loud chirp during dive display

species.confuser_notes: Rufous (seasonal visitor) has a simpler, lower-pitched buzz; Anna's song is longer and more complex

songs.location: Salem, Polk, Oregon
songs.remarks:
The same hummingbird male as on XC156822 successfully survived several sub-freezing cold nights and started his display song again after timeout he took for the cold weather. The only difference - previously he used high dead branch of Sequoia while currently he moved to the bush much closer to my heated  feeder.
High pass filter was used to remove street noise below 1.8kHz

songs.location: McClellan Ranch Preserve, Santa Clara, California
songs.remarks: Display flight above a *Quercus agrifolia* in an open field.  The loud squeak at the beginning of the cut is the mechanical sound produced by the tail feathers at the bottom of the dive.

songs.location: Sabino Canyon National Recreation Area, Pima County, Arizona
songs.remarks:
Trimmed and normalized.

Vocalizing from top of a mesquite bush. 

Dull, cloudy day. Song seemed quiet, but the recording was made nearly underneath the bird.

Arboretum Garden (near  Seattle), King County, Washington
Arboretum Garden (near  Seattle), King County, Washington

calls.location: Salem, Polk, Oregon
calls.remarks:
Anna's Hummingbird male perching on the bush between two houses.
Used equipment - Tascam DR-100, built-in unidirectional microphone. Record level normalized, high pass filter (2kHz) used to get rid of background street noise.
Video available on Youtube <http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=q85uU5EyRZg>
Photos available on Flickr <http://www.flickr.com/photos/bushman_k/11229411255/>

calls.location: Salem, Polk, Oregon
calls.remarks:
Recording level was normalized, no filtering applied. Used equipment - Tascam DR-100 with unidirectional built-in microphone. Some echo could be caused by parallel walls surrounding the recording location.

Female was sitting in the middle of dense bush (2m from the ground) between two houses and producing series of clicking sounds. 

This female visiting my feeder, located in 9 feet (3m) from bush mentioned above, many times every day at least for one month before this record was made. This is the first time, when I've noticed such a behavior. Male of the same species was humming around while female was singing.

Photo were taken right before the recording: <http://www.flickr.com/photos/bushman_k/10600919896/>

calls.location: West Sacramento, Yolo County, California
calls.remarks:
I modified the recording with Adobe Audition by shortening it so that I only preserved the territorial sound of the Anna’s Hummingbird’s tail chirp. I lowered background noise too, especially below 1k. (It was a windy day.)
This hummingbird was flying diving between hedgerows south of the Stonegate Pond and east of the Clarksburg Trail. 
The hummingbird flew 10 meters or so, then shot to the bottom of his arc where his tail feathers created the high-pitched, high-volume chirp. I don’t know if he did it because I was there or he was courting a female or defending his turf.

calls.location: Swan Lake, Victoria, Vancouver Island, BC
calls.remarks: Two sets of calls from single male perched at top of small tree on southern side Swan Lake sanctuary.  About a dozen male Anna's hummingbirds seen and only two females spotted in an hour and half later afternoon birding.

calls.location: Cape Flattery, Neah Bay, Clallam County, Washington
calls.remarks: Point-blank calls... I was searching for a rare bird, and had ducked into a large bush. While in the bush, this Chestnut-backed Chickadee landed about 2 feet to the left of my ear, and I froze and quietly hit "record" on my Olympus, which I'd had in hand the whole time. Habitat: tall evergreens with low brush on high, rocky cliffs above the ocean. Migrant trap. Weather: mild, sunny. HPF used.

songs.location: Swan Lake, Victoria, Vancouver Island, BC
songs.remarks:
Calls and songs from several individuals feeding on ground and close to ground in bushes in flooded wetlands.

This recording was made on the northern edge of Swan Lake, two days after heavy rains flooded the lake's surrounding grasslands.  Main trails were still flooded and many bird species moved out of lake areas into surrounding flooded grasslands and forests.  At least six Virginia Rails were observed in one hour in the flooded areas, which is a larger number that usually seen.

confuser_pairs.label: Black-capped vs Chestnut-backed Chickadee
confuser_pairs.key_difference: Black-capped: slower dee-dee-dee + clear fee-bee song. Chestnut-backed: faster, higher, no fee-bee.

confuser_pairs.label: Song Sparrow vs Golden-crowned Sparrow
confuser_pairs.key_difference: Song Sparrow: 3 notes + complex trill. Golden-crowned: 3 pure descending whistles, no trill.

confuser_pairs.label: American Crow vs Steller's Jay
confuser_pairs.key_difference: Crow: clean caw-caw. Jay: harsh shack-shack, more grating and varied.

confuser_pairs.label: Dark-eyed Junco vs Spotted Towhee
confuser_pairs.key_difference: Junco: even trill on one pitch. Towhee: 2 intro notes THEN a trill.

confuser_pairs.label: House Finch vs Song Sparrow
confuser_pairs.key_difference: House Finch: continuous warble ending in buzz. Song Sparrow: structured 3-note opening.

lesson_plan.description: 5 lessons of 3 birds each, ordered by distinctiveness (most distinctive first)

lessons.title: The unmistakable three
lessons.rationale: Loud, distinctive, impossible to confuse with each other. Builds confidence.

lessons.title: Backyard singers
lessons.rationale: Classic songs you'll hear every morning. Introduces the fee-bee mnemonic.

lessons.title: The sparrow start
lessons.rationale: First confuser challenge — three sparrow-like species with distinct songs.

lessons.title: Small and buzzy
lessons.rationale: Complex songs that reward repeated listening. Wren vs finch structure.

lessons.title: Flock sounds and mimics
lessons.rationale: Contact calls and mimicry. Introduces the chickadee confuser pair.
